reasoning: 2023 featured a developing El Niño and became the hottest year on record. 2024 saw the El Niño peak, keeping temperatures extremely high. Historically, the year following an El Niño peak experiences a shift to neutral or La Niña conditions, resulting in a cooling effect on global average temperatures that outweighs the annual secular warming trend (typically 0.1-0.2 °C cooling from ENSO vs. ~0.02-0.03 °C secular warming). Climate models in early 2024 predicted a transition to La Niña by late 2024, meaning 2025 will very likely be influenced by La Niña's cooling effect. Because of this cyclical cooling, the chance of 2025 breaking the temperature records set in 2023 and 2024 is quite low. While an anomalous acceleration in global warming (e.g., from shipping aerosol regulation or other feedback loops) makes the probability higher than historical La Niña baseline years, it remains firmly constrained.
